Upload: `_, err = client.PutObject(ctx, &s3.PutObjectInput{ Bucket: aws.String(bucket), Key: aws.String(key), Body: bytes.NewReader(data), ContentType: aws.String("application/json") })`. Download: `result, err := client.GetObject(ctx, &s3.GetObjectInput{Bucket: &bucket, Key: &key}); defer result.Body.Close(); io.Copy(w, result.Body)`. Presigned URL: `presignClient := s3.NewPresignClient(client); url, _ := presignClient.PresignGetObject(ctx, &s3.GetObjectInput{...}, s3.WithPresignExpires(15*time.Minute))`. UNSAID: `manager.NewUploader(client)` for multipart uploads (files > 5MB): `uploader.Upload(ctx, &s3.PutObjectInput{Body: file})`. `manager.NewDownloader(client)` for concurrent part download. AWS SDK v2 uses `aws.String(s)` and `aws.Int64(n)` helpers — all pointer fields. `AWS_REGION`, `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` env vars or IAM role for credentials.

Go `html/template` and `text/template` for rendering: `tmpl, err := template.ParseFiles("base.html", "content.html"); tmpl.Execute(w, data)`. `html/template` auto-escapes HTML — prevents XSS. `text/template` does NOT escape — use only for non-HTML output (config files, code generation). `template.FuncMap` for custom functions: `tmpl.Funcs(template.FuncMap{"upper": strings.ToUpper})`. Template actions: `{{.FieldName}}`, `{{if .Condition}}...{{end}}`, `{{range .Slice}}...{{end}}`, `{{with .Value}}...{{end}}`. UNSAID: `html/template.HTML(str)` marks a string as safe HTML — only use for HTML you generated, never for user input. Template parsing is expensive — cache parsed templates in production. `template.Must(template.ParseGlob("templates/*.html"))` panics on parse error — appropriate for startup validation. Embedded templates: `//go:embed templates/*; var tmplFS embed.FS; template.ParseFS(tmplFS, "templates/*.html")` — includes templates in the binary.

Go `append` and capacity growth rate: `append` doubles capacity when the current slice is full (for small slices; for large slices it grows by ~25%). Each doubling allocates a new backing array and copies all elements — O(n) total work amortized over n appends, but individual worst-case appends are O(n). For building large slices: `pre-allocate with make([]T, 0, estimatedSize)` to avoid repeated reallocations. Common failure: `for i := 0; i < 1000000; i++ { s = append(s, item) }` without pre-allocation — ~20 reallocations and copies for 1M items. UNSAID: `copy(dst, src)` always copies `min(len(dst), len(src))` elements — ensure dst is the right size. `append(a, b...)` appends all elements of slice b to a. `s = s[:0]` resets length to 0 while keeping capacity (reuses underlying array) — use for resetting a reusable slice buffer. `slices.Grow(s, n)` (Go 1.21+) hints that n additional elements will be appended — preallocates without changing length.

Go `sqlc` for type-safe SQL queries: define queries in `.sql` files with annotations: `-- name: GetUser :one; SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE id = $1`. Run `sqlc generate` to produce Go code: `GetUser(ctx, id)` returns a typed `User` struct. `-- name: ListUsers :many` returns `[]User`. `-- name: CreateUser :exec` executes without return. UNSAID: `sqlc` reads your database schema and query files at code generation time — changes to schema require regeneration. `sqlc.yaml` configures the generator: schema files, query files, output package. `sqlc` validates SQL syntax and argument types against the schema at generation time — SQL errors are compile-time errors (via generation). Unlike GORM: no N+1 query risk — you write explicit SQL. For complex queries: `sqlc` supports CTEs, window functions, any valid SQL. Pair with `pgx` or `database/sql` drivers. `go generate ./...` re-runs `sqlc generate` as part of the build process.

Go `net/url` and URL construction: `u := &url.URL{ Scheme: "https", Host: "api.example.com", Path: "/users" }; q := u.Query(); q.Set("page", "1"); u.RawQuery = q.Encode()`. Parsing: `u, err := url.Parse(rawURL)`. `url.PathEscape(s)` encodes path segments; `url.QueryEscape(s)` for query values. `url.Values` for building query strings: `params := url.Values{"q": {"search term"}, "page": {"1"}}; params.Encode()`. UNSAID: `url.Parse` does NOT validate that the URL is a valid HTTP URL — it parses structure only. Use `u.Hostname()` (strips brackets from IPv6) vs `u.Host` (may include port). `url.ParseRequestURI(s)` validates the URL is a valid request URI (no relative references). For SSRF prevention: always validate parsed URLs against an allowlist of schemes and hosts before making requests. `url.URL.JoinPath(elem...)` (Go 1.19+) safely joins path elements — prevents path traversal better than string concatenation.

Go `init()` function order dependency: `init()` functions in a package run before `main()`, after variable initialization. Multiple `init()` in a file run in order of appearance. Across files: init order follows file name order (not guaranteed by spec). Common failure: `init()` functions with inter-package dependencies — package A's init relies on package B's init completing first. Go guarantees package-level `init()` dependency order based on import graph, but within a package, file order is implementation-defined. UNSAID: Each file can have multiple `init()` functions (legal). `init()` cannot be called or referenced — it's automatically invoked. Side-effect imports `import _ "pkg"` register drivers, codecs, etc. via their `init()`. Overuse of `init()` makes startup order difficult to understand — prefer explicit initialization in `main()` over complex `init()` chains. Global variables initialized in `init()` are effectively mutable global state — harder to test.

Go `rate` limiting with `golang.org/x/time/rate`: `limiter := rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/100), 10)` — 100 req/s with burst of 10. `limiter.Wait(ctx)` blocks until a token is available (respects context cancellation). `limiter.Allow()` returns immediately (for non-blocking rate limiting). `limiter.Reserve()` returns a `Reservation` for scheduled execution. Per-key limiting: use a map of limiters: `limiters sync.Map; l, _ := limiters.LoadOrStore(ip, rate.NewLimiter(10, 1)); l.(*rate.Limiter).Wait(ctx)`. UNSAID: `rate.NewLimiter(rate.Inf, 0)` creates an unlimited rate — useful for test/debug modes. `rate.Limit(n)` means n tokens per second; `rate.Every(d)` is 1 token per d duration. For distributed rate limiting across instances: use Redis with atomic incr + TTL — `golang.org/x/time/rate` is in-memory only. Burst parameter: allows bursting up to N tokens before the steady-state rate kicks in — set to 1 for strict per-request limiting.

Go `unsafe` package and its risks: `unsafe.Pointer` bypasses type safety — allows pointer conversions between arbitrary types. `uintptr` is an integer large enough to hold a pointer but NOT a GC-tracked reference — a `uintptr` can become a dangling pointer if the GC moves the object. Rules: convert `unsafe.Pointer` to `uintptr` only within a single expression (not stored). `unsafe.Sizeof(x)` and `unsafe.Offsetof(s.f)` are safe for struct layout inspection. `unsafe.Slice(ptr, len)` and `unsafe.String(ptr, len)` (Go 1.17+) create slices/strings from pointer+length. UNSAID: `//go:linkname` in the `unsafe` package allows accessing unexported symbols from other packages — widely considered a hack, breaks on runtime updates. `unsafe.SliceData(s)` and `unsafe.StringData(s)` (Go 1.20+) return the backing array pointer — safer than `(*reflect.SliceHeader)(unsafe.Pointer(&s)).Data`. `go vet` warns about some unsafe usages. The standard library uses `unsafe` internally — it's not forbidden, but requires extra care and code review.

Go `fiber` framework patterns: `app := fiber.New(fiber.Config{ ErrorHandler: customErrorHandler, ReadTimeout: 15*time.Second, WriteTimeout: 15*time.Second })`. Route: `app.Get("/users/:id", func(c *fiber.Ctx) error { id := c.Params("id"); user, err := db.GetUser(id); if err != nil { return err }; return c.JSON(user) })`. Middleware: `app.Use(recover.New(), logger.New(), limiter.New())`. Custom error handler: `func customErrorHandler(c *fiber.Ctx, err error) error { code := fiber.StatusInternalServerError; if e, ok := err.(*fiber.Error); ok { code = e.Code }; return c.Status(code).JSON(fiber.Map{"error": err.Error()}) }`. UNSAID: Fiber uses `fasthttp` under the hood — up to 10x faster than `net/http` for simple handlers. But: `*fiber.Ctx` is NOT safe to use after the handler returns (fasthttp reuses it). Never pass `c *fiber.Ctx` to goroutines or store it. For async work from a handler: copy needed values from `c` before spawning a goroutine. `c.Locals(key, val)` stores request-scoped data; `c.Locals(key)` retrieves it — replaces `context.WithValue`.

Go `goroutine` and closure over loop variable (range over function, Go 1.23+): Go 1.23 introduced range-over-functions (`iter.Seq`). Range over channel: `for v := range ch { go func() { use(v) }() }` — before 1.22, v was shared; 1.22+ each iteration has a new variable. Range over slice/map: same behavior. Range over function (1.23+): `for v := range iter { go func() { use(v) }() }` — follows 1.22+ semantics (each iteration has its own variable). UNSAID: The most important change in Go 1.22: `for i, v := range slice { go func() { fmt.Println(i, v) }() }` is NOW safe — no need for `i := i; v := v` captures. However, you must be on Go 1.22+ toolchain AND your `go.mod` must specify `go 1.22` or later. If `go.mod` says `go 1.21`, the old (shared variable) behavior is preserved for backward compatibility. Always check your `go.mod` minimum version.

Go `wire` dependency injection: `wire` generates DI code at compile time — no runtime reflection. `//go:build wireinject` tag marks the injection file. Providers: `func NewRepository(db *sql.DB) *Repository { return &Repository{db: db} }`. Wire set: `var repoSet = wire.NewSet(NewRepository)`. Injector: `func InitializeApp() (*App, error) { wire.Build(repoSet, NewService, NewApp); return nil, nil }`. Run `wire` to generate `wire_gen.go`. UNSAID: `wire` traces the dependency graph and generates explicit constructor call chains in `wire_gen.go` — inspect the generated file to understand what's happening. `wire.Value(v)` provides a literal value; `wire.InterfaceValue` binds an interface to a concrete type. `wire.Bind((*MyInterface)(nil), (*MyImpl)(nil))` binds an interface to its implementation. No magic at runtime — the generated code is ordinary Go. Compared to fx (Uber's DI): wire is simpler (compile-time), fx is more powerful (runtime, module system). For most projects: wire is the right choice.

Go `net/http` server request body limits and parsing: `http.MaxBytesReader(w, r.Body, maxBytes)` wraps the request body with a size limit — returns `*http.MaxBytesError` when exceeded. `r.ParseForm()` parses URL-encoded body and query params. `r.ParseMultipartForm(maxMemory)` for multipart forms — files larger than `maxMemory` spill to disk. `r.FormValue(key)` is lazy (calls ParseForm). `r.FormFile(key)` for file uploads: returns `multipart.File` and `*multipart.FileHeader`. UNSAID: `http.MaxBytesReader` MUST be set before reading the body — it's a defense against request body attacks (huge body fills memory). Default: no limit. Recommended limit: `32 << 20` (32MB) for general APIs; lower for APIs not expecting file uploads. `r.Body.Close()` must be called — it's called automatically only when the request is fully handled by the server, not when you leave the handler. For JSON: `json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&v)` streams without loading into memory.

Go `database/sql` transaction isolation and retry: `tx, err := db.BeginTx(ctx, &sql.TxOptions{Isolation: sql.LevelSerializable})`. Serializable transactions may fail with a serialization error and MUST be retried. Common failure: wrapping a transaction in a function and returning the error without retrying serialization failures. Fix: `for { tx, _ := db.Begin(); err := doWork(tx); if err != nil { tx.Rollback(); if isSerializationError(err) { continue }; return err }; if err := tx.Commit(); err != nil { if isSerializationError(err) { continue }; return err }; break }`. UNSAID: Postgres serialization error: `pq.Error.Code == "40001"` or `pgx.PgError.Code == "40001"`. Read-committed (default) avoids serialization errors but allows phantom reads. For financial operations: use serializable transactions or explicit locks (`SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`). `db.BeginTx` vs `db.Begin`: always use `BeginTx` with a context — `Begin` ignores context cancellation during the transaction.

Go `iter` package (Go 1.23+) and range over functions: the standard `iter.Seq[V]` and `iter.Seq2[K, V]` types enable `range` over custom iterators. `func All(s []T) iter.Seq2[int, T] { return func(yield func(int, T) bool) { for i, v := range s { if !yield(i, v) { return } } } }`. Consuming: `for i, v := range All(items) { process(i, v) }`. `slices.All` from `slices` package (Go 1.23+) returns `iter.Seq2[int, T]`. `maps.All` for map iteration. `slices.Collect` converts `iter.Seq[T]` to `[]T`. UNSAID: The `yield` function returns `false` to signal early termination (`break` in the range loop). Custom iterators must NOT call `yield` after it returns `false` — undefined behavior. `iter.Pull` and `iter.Pull2` (push-to-pull conversion) allow consuming iterators from non-range contexts (like `for yield()` loops). This enables lazy pipeline composition without goroutines/channels overhead.

Go functional options pattern for constructor configuration: define an `Option` type `type Option func(*Config)` and expose option functions: `func WithTimeout(d time.Duration) Option { return func(c *Config) { c.Timeout = d } }`. Constructor accepts variadic options: `func NewClient(addr string, opts ...Option) *Client`. Apply with a loop: `for _, o := range opts { o(&cfg) }`. This avoids config structs with dozens of zero values and makes API evolvable without breaking changes — adding a new option never requires changes to existing callers. UNSAID: pair with unexported `config` struct and exported `Option` type. Use sensible defaults in the constructor before applying options. Libraries like `google.golang.org/grpc`, `go.uber.org/zap`, and `net/http` use this pattern extensively. For required parameters, keep them as positional args; only truly optional configuration goes in options. Avoid the anti-pattern of a public `Config` struct with exported fields — it bakes in field names as API surface.

Go `singleflight` deduplication for cache stampedes: `golang.org/x/sync/singleflight`. When multiple goroutines call an expensive function simultaneously, `singleflight` ensures only one call executes; others wait and receive the same result. Usage: `var g singleflight.Group; v, err, shared := g.Do(key, func() (any, error) { return fetchFromDB(ctx, key) })`. The `shared` bool indicates the result was shared. Use as a layer between cache miss and database fetch: cache miss → singleflight → (one DB call) → populate cache. Without singleflight, a cache expiry under high load triggers N simultaneous DB queries (thundering herd). UNSAID: the key should be the same string for logically identical requests. `g.DoChan` provides a non-blocking channel-based variant. `g.Forget(key)` removes a key mid-flight if needed. The group is not tied to any context — if the underlying call should respect ctx cancellation, handle it inside the function. For per-service singleflight, keep a `singleflight.Group` per service instance, not global.

FAILURE: Go fiber `*fiber.Ctx` reuse after handler returns. Fiber (gofiber/fiber) reuses `Ctx` objects via a sync.Pool for performance. If you store a `*fiber.Ctx` reference and use it after the handler returns (in a goroutine, deferred logger, etc.), you access a recycled context with different request data. Symptoms: logging shows wrong request path/body, middleware reads stale values, race detector reports data races. Fix: copy all values you need before the handler returns — `body := string(c.Body())`, `path := c.Path()`, `reqID := c.Get('X-Request-ID')`. For goroutines spawned from handlers, pass copied values, not the ctx. If you must pass context to async work, use `c.Context()` (the underlying `*fasthttp.RequestCtx`) only within the handler's lifetime. UNSAID: this is a fundamental design choice in Fiber for zero-allocation performance — it's documented but frequently misses developers from net/http backgrounds. The standard `net/http` handler does not reuse `*http.Request` objects.

Go `slices` package (stdlib, 1.21+) replaces manual slice utilities: `slices.Sort(s)`, `slices.SortFunc(s, cmp)`, `slices.Contains(s, v)`, `slices.Index(s, v)`, `slices.Compact(s)` (deduplicate adjacent), `slices.Reverse(s)`, `slices.Max(s)`, `slices.Min(s)`, `slices.Equal(a, b)`, `slices.Grow(s, n)`, `slices.Clip(s)`. `slices.SortFunc` uses `cmp.Compare` or a custom comparator returning negative/zero/positive int. `slices.BinarySearch(s, v)` returns (index, found) for sorted slices. `slices.Delete(s, i, j)` removes elements in range [i,j). UNSAID: before 1.21, these required `sort.Slice` with closures or third-party packages like `samber/lo`. `slices.Sort` is ~2x faster than `sort.Slice` due to type specialization. `slices.Compact` is especially useful after sorting to deduplicate. Use `cmp.Compare` from the `cmp` package for ordered types. The functions modify the slice in place (Sort, Reverse, Compact) or return a new slice (Grow, Clip, Delete returns modified slice).

Go `maps` package (stdlib, 1.21+) provides generic map utilities: `maps.Keys(m)` returns `[]K`, `maps.Values(m)` returns `[]V`, `maps.Copy(dst, src)` copies entries, `maps.Delete(m, keys...)` deletes multiple keys, `maps.Equal(m1, m2)` compares maps, `maps.Clone(m)` returns a shallow copy. All functions work with any `map[K]V` via generics — no type assertions needed. UNSAID: before 1.21, getting map keys required `for k := range m { keys = append(keys, k) }` loops. `maps.Keys` and `maps.Values` return slices in an unspecified order (map iteration is random) — sort afterward if order matters. `maps.Clone` is shallow — pointer values are not deep copied. For iteration in Go 1.23+, prefer `maps.All(m)` which returns an `iter.Seq2[K,V]` for use with range-over-func. `maps.Collect` (1.23+) builds a map from an iterator. These functions complement the `slices` package for complete generic collection utilities.

Go built-in `min` and `max` functions (1.21+) work on any ordered type — `min(a, b)`, `max(a, b)`, `min(a, b, c, d...)` with variadic support. Before 1.21, Go had no generic min/max; every codebase defined its own `func min(a, b int) int` which didn't work for float64, and `math.Min(float64, float64) float64` required type conversions. The new built-ins infer type from arguments and work with any `constraints.Ordered` type (integers, floats, strings). Same for `clear(m)` (1.21+) to clear a map or zero a slice. UNSAID: `min`/`max` are built-in identifiers, not imported from a package — no import needed. They shadow any local `min`/`max` functions if redefined. `min` on strings does lexicographic comparison. The variadic form `min(vals...)` works correctly. These additions reduce a significant source of boilerplate in Go codebases. `clear(s)` on a slice zeros all elements but retains length, unlike `s = s[:0]` which retains capacity.

Go fuzz testing with `go test -fuzz`: define a fuzz target `func FuzzParseInput(f *testing.F)`. Seed the corpus: `f.Add("example input", 42)`. Write the fuzz function: `f.Fuzz(func(t *testing.T, s string, n int) { result, err := ParseInput(s, n); if err == nil && !validateResult(result) { t.Fail() } })`. Run with `go test -fuzz=FuzzParseInput -fuzztime=60s`. Found inputs that trigger failures are saved in `testdata/fuzz/FuzzParseInput/`. These become permanent regression tests — run without `-fuzz` flag to replay them. UNSAID: fuzzing requires Go 1.18+. The fuzzer mutates seed inputs systematically using coverage-guided mutation. Fuzz functions must be deterministic — no random number generation inside. Only certain types are supported as fuzz arguments: string, []byte, int, int64, float64, bool, rune, byte, uint. Fuzzing is especially valuable for parsers, decoders, hash functions, and any function operating on untrusted input. The `-fuzz` flag runs indefinitely unless `-fuzztime` or `-fuzzminimizetime` is specified.

Go `go tool trace` for goroutine scheduling and latency analysis: instrument with `runtime/trace`: `trace.Start(f); defer trace.Stop()`. Run program, then `go tool trace trace.out` opens a browser UI showing: goroutine lifecycle, GC events, heap size, network blocking, syscall blocking, scheduler latency. The timeline view shows goroutine scheduling gaps — if goroutines are blocked for long periods, identify the cause (lock contention, syscall, GC stop-the-world). `GOTRACE=1` environment variable enables trace without code changes in some scenarios. UNSAID: trace overhead is significant — only enable for profiling sessions, not production. For production latency analysis, prefer `net/http/pprof` or OpenTelemetry spans. The trace viewer requires Chrome/Chromium for full functionality. Use `go tool trace -http=:6060 trace.out` to serve the UI. GC pause times appear clearly in the trace — if stop-the-world pauses exceed 1ms, investigate heap growth patterns. Trace files grow quickly — limit trace duration to seconds for manageable file sizes.

Go protobuf with `buf` toolchain: define `.proto` files, use `buf.yaml` for module configuration and `buf.gen.yaml` for code generation. `buf generate` produces Go structs with `protoc-gen-go` and gRPC service stubs with `protoc-gen-go-grpc`. `buf lint` enforces style rules (field numbering, naming conventions, package structure). `buf breaking` detects breaking API changes between proto versions — wire-compatible but semantically breaking changes. `buf dep update` manages proto dependencies from the Buf Schema Registry. UNSAID: `buf` replaces the fragile `protoc` + plugin ecosystem with a reproducible, dependency-managed build. Proto field numbers are permanent — removing or renumbering breaks wire compatibility. Use `reserved` to retire field numbers safely. `google.protobuf.Timestamp` for time, not `int64` epoch. `optional` keyword in proto3 distinguishes field presence from zero value. For large services, consider separate `buf` modules for internal vs public APIs. `connect-go` (connectrpc/connect-go) is a modern alternative to `grpc-go` that works with plain HTTP/1.1.

Go `ent` ORM for schema-as-code: define entities in `ent/schema/` as Go structs with `Fields()` and `Edges()` methods. Run `go generate ./ent` to generate a full type-safe client. Query: `client.User.Query().Where(user.Name("alice")).Only(ctx)`. Create: `client.User.Create().SetName("bob").Save(ctx)`. Edges (relations): `client.User.QueryPosts(u).All(ctx)`. Schema migrations: `client.Schema.Create(ctx)` for auto-migrate or generate migration files with `entc`. UNSAID: `ent` is developed and used by Meta/Facebook at scale. Schema changes require running `go generate` — the generated code will not compile if schema and generated files are out of sync, which is a useful safety property. `ent` supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and CockroachDB. For production: use `entc` migration files rather than auto-migrate. Avoid `entc.WithGlobalID()` if you need predictable ID sequences. The generated code is verbose but auditable — you can always read what SQL it generates. Unlike GORM, `ent` prevents implicit N+1 via explicit edge loading.

Go NATS messaging with `nats.go`: connect with `nats.Connect(nats.DefaultURL)`. Publish: `nc.Publish("subject", data)`. Subscribe: `nc.Subscribe("subject", func(m *nats.Msg) { ... })`. Request-reply: `msg, err := nc.Request("service", data, timeout)`. Queue groups for load balancing: `nc.QueueSubscribe("subject", "workers", handler)` — only one member of the queue group receives each message. JetStream for persistence: `js, _ := nc.JetStream(); js.Publish("ORDERS.new", data)`. UNSAID: NATS core is at-most-once delivery. For at-least-once, use JetStream with `AckExplicit` and `msg.Ack()`. JetStream consumers track acknowledged messages and redeliver unacked messages after `AckWait` duration. NATS subjects use dot-separated tokens: `orders.created`, wildcards `orders.*` (single token) or `orders.>` (all tokens). NATS is significantly simpler and faster than Kafka for scenarios that don't require long-term log retention. For Go microservices, NATS JetStream covers most messaging needs with lower operational overhead than Kafka.

Go `go generate` pattern for code generation: add `//go:generate` comments in source files: `//go:generate mockgen -source=service.go -destination=mock_service.go`. Run all generators: `go generate ./...`. Common uses: `stringer` for enum String() methods, `mockgen` for interface mocks, `sqlc generate` for SQL, `ent` for ORM, `buf generate` for protobuf, `go-bindata` for embedding. In Makefile: `generate: go generate ./...`. UNSAID: `go generate` is not run automatically by `go build` — it must be invoked explicitly. Generated files should be committed to version control (not in `.gitignore`) so that builds work without running generators — unless the team has generator tooling reliably installed in CI. Add a `//go:generate` comment near the type or interface that the generated code relates to. Use `//go:build ignore` to exclude generator scripts from normal compilation. Document in CONTRIBUTING.md which generators are needed and how to install them.

FAILURE: Go SQL injection via `fmt.Sprintf` query construction. Using `fmt.Sprintf("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '%s'", userInput)` or string concatenation for query parameters allows SQL injection. Symptoms: data exfiltration, data deletion, authentication bypass. Fix: always use parameterized queries with `$1/$2` placeholders (PostgreSQL) or `?` placeholders (MySQL/SQLite): `db.QueryContext(ctx, "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = $1", userInput)`. For dynamic `IN` clauses with variable-length slices, build the placeholder string programmatically: `placeholders := make([]string, len(ids)); for i := range ids { placeholders[i] = fmt.Sprintf("$%d", i+1) }; query := "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (" + strings.Join(placeholders, ",") + ")"`. UNSAID: this is OWASP A03:2021. ORMs like GORM and sqlc use parameterization by default, but GORM's `Raw()` and `Where()` with raw strings bypass this protection. Even with ORMs, never pass user input as raw SQL fragments. Use `pgx.Identifier` for dynamic table/column names (rare, high-risk case).

Go TLS configuration for production servers: use `tls.Config` with `MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS12` (never TLS 1.0/1.1). For modern servers targeting browsers: `MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS13`. Certificate loading: `tls.LoadX509KeyPair(certFile, keyFile)`. For mutual TLS (mTLS): set `ClientAuth: tls.RequireAndVerifyClientCert` and `ClientCAs` pool. Use `autocert` package for automatic Let's Encrypt certificates: `autocert.Manager{Prompt: autocert.AcceptTOS, HostPolicy: autocert.HostWhitelist(domain)}`. UNSAID: never set `InsecureSkipVerify: true` in production — it disables certificate validation entirely and enables MITM. Custom `RootCAs` pool is needed when connecting to services with private CAs (Kubernetes in-cluster, internal services). `tls.Config` is safe to share across connections after construction. For gRPC: use `credentials.NewTLS(tlsConfig)`. Cipher suite selection: Go's defaults are sensible; only customize if compliance requires specific suites. `GODEBUG=tlsrsakex=1` re-enables RSA key exchange for legacy compatibility.

Go CORS handling with `rs/cors` package: `c := cors.New(cors.Options{AllowedOrigins: []string{"https://example.com"}, AllowedMethods: []string{"GET","POST","PUT","DELETE"}, AllowedHeaders: []string{"Authorization","Content-Type"}, AllowCredentials: true, MaxAge: 86400})`. Wrap handler: `handler = c.Handler(mux)`. For development: `cors.AllowAll()`. UNSAID: never use `AllowedOrigins: []string{"*"}` with `AllowCredentials: true` — browsers will block it (CORS spec violation). Wildcard origins are fine for public APIs without credentials. Preflight (`OPTIONS`) requests are handled automatically by the middleware. If deploying behind a load balancer/CDN, ensure the `Origin` header is not stripped. For chi: `r.Use(cors.Handler(cors.Options{...}))`. For Fiber: use `fiber/middleware/cors`. Custom origin validation: `AllowOriginFunc: func(origin string) bool { return strings.HasSuffix(origin, ".example.com") }`. CORS is a browser security feature — it does not protect server-to-server API calls.

Go health check patterns for Kubernetes: expose `/healthz` (liveness) and `/readyz` (readiness) endpoints on a separate port (e.g., 8081) distinct from the application port (8080). Liveness: returns 200 if the process is alive and not deadlocked. Readiness: checks downstream dependencies (DB ping, cache connectivity) and returns 503 if not ready to serve. Implementation: `http.HandleFunc("/readyz", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { if err := db.PingContext(r.Context()); err != nil { http.Error(w, err.Error(), http.StatusServiceUnavailable); return }; w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK) })`. UNSAID: liveness and readiness must be separate — a readiness failure removes the pod from service load balancing but does not restart it. A liveness failure restarts the pod. Never check downstream dependencies in liveness (only in readiness) — if a transient DB hiccup causes liveness failure, pods restart in a cascade. Startup probe for slow-starting services: separate from liveness. Use `grpc_health_v1` for gRPC services. Libraries: `alexliesenfeld/health` provides circuit-breaker-aware health checks.

Go `go:embed` for static assets and templates: `//go:embed static/*` embeds all files in the `static` directory into the binary. Access via `embed.FS`: `var staticFiles embed.FS; content, _ := staticFiles.ReadFile("static/index.html")`. Serve embedded files: `http.FileServer(http.FS(staticFiles))`. For templates: `var templates embed.FS; tmpl := template.Must(template.New("").ParseFS(templates, "templates/*.html"))`. Embed single file: `//go:embed VERSION; var version string`. UNSAID: `//go:embed` directives must immediately precede the `var` declaration. Embedded files are read-only at runtime — you cannot write to an embedded FS. For `embed.FS` serving with a prefix, use `fs.Sub(staticFiles, "static")` to strip the directory prefix. Hidden files (starting with `.`) and files in hidden directories are not embedded by default — use `//go:embed all:static` to include them. Embedded content increases binary size — consider this for large asset collections. `go:embed` works with `string`, `[]byte`, and `embed.FS` variable types.

Go retry with exponential backoff: use `cenkalti/backoff/v4` package. Constant backoff: `backoff.Retry(operation, backoff.NewConstantBackOff(time.Second))`. Exponential: `b := backoff.NewExponentialBackOff(); b.InitialInterval = 500*time.Millisecond; b.MaxInterval = 30*time.Second; b.MaxElapsedTime = 2*time.Minute; backoff.Retry(op, b)`. With context: `backoff.RetryNotify(op, backoff.WithContext(b, ctx), func(err error, d time.Duration) { log.Printf("retrying after %v: %v", d, err) })`. Return `backoff.Permanent(err)` to stop retrying on non-retryable errors. UNSAID: without jitter, retries from multiple clients synchronize (retry storm). Use `backoff.NewExponentialBackOffWithInitialInterval` or add jitter manually. Only retry idempotent operations — retrying a non-idempotent write can cause duplicate effects. Implement retry-budget thinking: limit total retry rate across all clients, not just per-client. For HTTP clients, retry on 429 (respect `Retry-After` header), 502, 503, 504 — not on 4xx client errors.

Go circuit breaker with `sony/gobreaker`: `cb := gobreaker.NewCircuitBreaker(gobreaker.Settings{Name: "database", MaxRequests: 5, Interval: 10*time.Second, Timeout: 30*time.Second, ReadyToTrip: func(counts gobreaker.Counts) bool { return counts.ConsecutiveFailures > 5 }})`. Wrap calls: `result, err := cb.Execute(func() (any, error) { return db.QueryContext(ctx, query) })`. Circuit states: Closed (normal), Open (failing — fast-fail without calling), Half-Open (testing recovery). UNSAID: the circuit breaker prevents cascade failures — when a downstream service is degraded, fast-failing immediately instead of waiting for timeouts prevents thread/goroutine exhaustion in the calling service. `Timeout` is how long the circuit stays Open before transitioning to Half-Open. `MaxRequests` is the number of requests allowed in Half-Open state to probe recovery. `Interval` is the window for counting failures in Closed state. For HTTP services, combine with retry (retry inside circuit breaker = retry counts as one attempt toward the CB). Separate circuit breakers per downstream service.

FAILURE: Go `sync.Cond` broadcast-before-wait race condition. When using `sync.Cond` for condition variables, the pattern must be: lock mutex, check condition in a loop, wait if not met. `cond.L.Lock(); for !condition { cond.Wait() }; cond.L.Unlock()`. The failure: checking condition without the lock, or using `if` instead of `for`: `if !condition { cond.Wait() }` — a broadcast between the check and the wait is lost, causing indefinite blocking. A spurious wakeup (allowed by `sync.Cond`) also requires the loop. `cond.Broadcast()` must be called after setting the condition that goroutines are waiting on. UNSAID: in most Go code, channels are preferable to `sync.Cond` — use `chan struct{}` for one-shot signals, `sync.WaitGroup` for waiting on multiple operations. `sync.Cond` is appropriate for fan-out notifications to many goroutines (Broadcast), which channels cannot do efficiently. `cond.Signal()` wakes one waiting goroutine; `cond.Broadcast()` wakes all. Never call `cond.Wait()` without holding the associated lock — it will panic or cause undefined behavior.

Go `context.WithCancelCause` (1.21+) for structured cancellation: `ctx, cancel := context.WithCancelCause(parent)`. Cancel with a reason: `cancel(ErrShutdown)`. Read cause: `context.Cause(ctx)` returns the cause error. Before 1.21, all cancellations looked identical — `ctx.Err()` returned `context.Canceled` with no additional information. `WithCancelCause` enables: `if cause := context.Cause(ctx); errors.Is(cause, ErrTimeout) { ... } else if errors.Is(cause, ErrShutdown) { ... }`. For `errgroup` with cause: use `golang.org/x/sync/errgroup` which propagates the first non-nil error as the cancellation cause. UNSAID: `context.Cause` returns nil if context was cancelled without a cause (regular `cancel()` or deadline exceeded). This is backward compatible — old code using `ctx.Err()` still works. `cancel(nil)` cancels with a nil cause, making `context.Cause` return `context.Canceled`. Use `WithCancelCause` in long-running pipeline stages to communicate which stage failed and why without wrapping errors through return values.

Go OpenAPI code generation with `oapi-codegen`: write `openapi.yaml` spec first, generate server stubs and client code with `oapi-codegen -generate types,server,client -package api openapi.yaml > api/api.gen.go`. The generated server interface must be implemented by your handler struct. This ensures your implementation matches the spec. Alternatively, generate from Go types using `swaggo/swag` annotations: `// @Summary Get user; // @Param id path int true "User ID"`. Run `swag init` to generate `docs/swagger.json`. UNSAID: spec-first (`oapi-codegen`) is preferable for API design — the spec is the contract, not an afterthought. Code-first (`swag`) is faster for existing codebases. `oapi-codegen` supports multiple routing frameworks: chi, echo, gin, fiber. The generated types use nullable wrappers for optional fields — beware of `*string` vs `string` in generated code. For request validation, combine with `getkin/kin-openapi` for runtime request/response validation against the spec. Keep the OpenAPI spec in version control and validate it in CI with `spectral lint`.

FAILURE: Go `timer.Stop()` return value ignored, causing goroutine leak. `t := time.AfterFunc(duration, fn)` and `t.Stop()` returns false if the timer has already expired and the function has been called or is currently running. If you stop a timer and then reset it without draining: `t.Stop(); t.Reset(d)` — if Stop returned false, the channel from `time.NewTimer` still has an unread value, and the next Reset does not drain it. Correct pattern for `*time.Timer` channel drain: `if !t.Stop() { select { case <-t.C: default: } }; t.Reset(d)`. For `time.AfterFunc`, Stop is safe to call multiple times. UNSAID: this is a long-standing Go footgun documented since Go 1. `time.NewTicker` has the same issue with `Reset`. In Go 1.23+, `Timer.Reset` no longer requires draining — the timer channel is now drained automatically. For Go < 1.23, always check Stop return value. If you only need one-shot timers without reset, prefer `time.AfterFunc` over `time.NewTimer` — simpler, no channel to drain.

Go `golang-jwt/v5` JWT authentication: generate token: `claims := jwt.MapClaims{"sub": userID, "exp": jwt.NewNumericDate(time.Now().Add(24*time.Hour))}; token := jwt.NewWithClaims(jwt.SigningMethodHS256, claims); signed, _ := token.SignedString([]byte(secret))`. Validate: `token, err := jwt.Parse(signed, func(t *jwt.Token) (any, error) { if _, ok := t.Method.(*jwt.SigningMethodHMAC); !ok { return nil, fmt.Errorf("unexpected signing method") }; return []byte(secret), nil })`. UNSAID: always check the signing method in the key function — the `alg:none` attack allows forging tokens if the method is not validated. Use RS256 (RSA) or ES256 (ECDSA) for distributed systems where multiple services verify tokens without sharing a secret. Store JWTs in httpOnly cookies for browser apps, not localStorage — XSS cannot access httpOnly cookies. Set short expiry (15 min) with refresh tokens for sensitive operations. Never put sensitive data in JWT payload — it is base64 encoded, not encrypted. Use `jwt.RegisteredClaims` struct instead of `MapClaims` for type safety.

Go zero-downtime deployment with rolling restarts: signal handling for graceful shutdown is the foundation. Kubernetes rolling deploy: set `maxUnavailable: 0` and `maxSurge: 1` in deployment spec. Add `terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60` and a `preStop` sleep hook to allow load balancer deregistration before SIGTERM. In the application: `sigCh := make(chan os.Signal, 1); signal.Notify(sigCh, syscall.SIGTERM, syscall.SIGINT); <-sigCh; srv.Shutdown(ctx)`. For database migrations: ensure migrations are backward compatible — the old version must run alongside the new version during the rolling window. UNSAID: Kubernetes sends SIGTERM and then waits `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` before SIGKILL. Without `preStop` sleep, the pod receives SIGTERM while still in the load balancer — in-flight requests are dropped. Typical `preStop` sleep: 5-15 seconds matching the load balancer health check interval. Zero-downtime requires: the application drains connections on SIGTERM, the health check reports unhealthy before shutdown starts, and the deployment strategy respects the health check.

Go structured error types with HTTP status mapping: define domain errors as sentinel values or types, then map to HTTP status in handler layer. Pattern: `type AppError struct { Code string; Message string; HTTPStatus int; Err error }`. Or use typed errors: `var ErrNotFound = errors.New("not found")`. In HTTP handler: `var notFound *NotFoundError; if errors.As(err, &notFound) { http.Error(w, notFound.Message, http.StatusNotFound); return }`. Centralize error-to-status mapping: `func httpStatus(err error) int { switch { case errors.Is(err, ErrNotFound): return 404; case errors.Is(err, ErrForbidden): return 403; ... } }`. UNSAID: never expose internal error messages to clients — log the full error server-side, return a sanitized message to clients. Use error codes (strings like "USER_NOT_FOUND") in API responses to enable client-side internationalization. `errors.Is` traverses the error chain via `Unwrap()` — wrapped errors are matched. Returning 500 for all errors is a common failure mode that makes debugging impossible — invest in proper error classification early.

FAILURE: Go `strings.Builder` or `bytes.Buffer` concurrent write without synchronization. Both `strings.Builder` and `bytes.Buffer` are not safe for concurrent use. Writing to them from multiple goroutines causes data races, undefined behavior, and corrupted output. Symptoms: garbled output in logs, go race detector reports, random test failures. Fix: either protect with a `sync.Mutex`, or use one builder per goroutine and merge results after, or use `sync.Pool` for per-goroutine builders. For concurrent logging to a single output, use a `sync.Mutex`-protected writer or a channel-based writer. UNSAID: `bytes.Buffer` documentation explicitly states it is not safe for concurrent use. `strings.Builder` similarly. The standard logger (`log.Logger`) is goroutine-safe because it uses an internal mutex. `sync.Pool` with `strings.Builder` is a common pattern for high-throughput string building across goroutines: get a builder from the pool, write to it, return it to the pool after use (reset with `b.Reset()`). `io.Pipe` provides a goroutine-safe pipe for streaming data between goroutines.

Go `aws-sdk-go-v2` S3 operations: load config: `cfg, _ := config.LoadDefaultConfig(ctx, config.WithRegion("us-east-1"))`. Create client: `client := s3.NewFromConfig(cfg)`. Upload: `client.PutObject(ctx, &s3.PutObjectInput{Bucket: aws.String(bucket), Key: aws.String(key), Body: reader, ContentType: aws.String("application/json")})`. Download: `result, _ := client.GetObject(ctx, &s3.GetObjectInput{Bucket: aws.String(bucket), Key: aws.String(key)}); defer result.Body.Close()`. List: `client.ListObjectsV2(ctx, &s3.ListObjectsV2Input{Bucket: aws.String(bucket), Prefix: aws.String(prefix)})`. For large files: use `manager.NewUploader(client)` with multipart upload. UNSAID: always close `result.Body` to avoid connection leaks. Use `manager.NewUploader` for files > 5MB — it automatically handles multipart upload. For presigned URLs: `presignClient := s3.NewPresignClient(client); req, _ := presignClient.PresignGetObject(ctx, input, func(o *s3.PresignOptions) { o.Expires = 15*time.Minute })`. Credentials are loaded from environment (`AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`), instance metadata, or ECS task role automatically.

Go `encoding/json` streaming for large payloads: `json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(v)` writes directly to an `io.Writer` without buffering the entire JSON in memory. `json.NewDecoder(r).Decode(&v)` reads from an `io.Reader` incrementally. For newline-delimited JSON (JSONL): loop with `dec.Decode(&obj)` until `io.EOF`. For JSON streaming arrays: use `dec.Token()` to read `[`, then loop `dec.More()` with `dec.Decode(&item)`, then read `]`. UNSAID: `json.Marshal`/`json.Unmarshal` require the entire payload in memory — problematic for GB-scale JSON. `json.Decoder.Buffered()` returns unread data after `Decode`. For highest performance: `json-iterator/go` is a drop-in replacement 3-5x faster than `encoding/json`, or `goccy/go-json` which is even faster and uses the standard `encoding/json` API. `sonic` (bytedance) is the fastest but requires CGO. For custom JSON handling: implement `json.Marshaler` and `json.Unmarshaler` interfaces with `MarshalJSON()` and `UnmarshalJSON([]byte)` methods.

Go `sync.Map` for concurrent read-heavy map with infrequent writes: `var m sync.Map`. Store: `m.Store(key, value)`. Load: `v, ok := m.Load(key)`. LoadOrStore: `actual, loaded := m.LoadOrStore(key, value)`. Delete: `m.Delete(key)`. Range: `m.Range(func(k, v any) bool { ...; return true })`. UNSAID: `sync.Map` is optimized for two specific cases: (1) a key written once and read many times (cache pattern), (2) multiple goroutines each operating on disjoint sets of keys. For general-purpose concurrent maps with mixed read/write patterns, a `map[K]V` protected by `sync.RWMutex` is often faster due to lower per-operation overhead. `sync.Map` has no `Len()` method — use `Range` with a counter if needed (expensive). Type assertions are required since values are `any`. `LoadOrStore` is the atomic check-and-set primitive — do not use Load followed by Store as separate operations (TOCTOU race). In Go 1.23+, `sync.Map` gained typed `sync.Map[K, V]` via generics — use `go.uber.org/atomic` or `xsync` for typed concurrent maps in earlier versions.

Go `go-playground/validator/v10` for struct validation: tag structs with `validate:` annotations: `type User struct { Name string `validate:"required,min=2,max=50"`; Email string `validate:"required,email"`; Age int `validate:"gte=0,lte=120"` }`. Validate: `v := validator.New(); err := v.Struct(user)`. Extract field errors: `var validationErrors validator.ValidationErrors; if errors.As(err, &validationErrors) { for _, fe := range validationErrors { fmt.Printf("%s: %s", fe.Field(), fe.Tag()) } }`. Custom validators: `v.RegisterValidation("strong-password", validateStrongPassword)`. UNSAID: register `v.RegisterTagNameFunc` to use JSON field names in errors instead of struct field names — important for API error messages. `validate:"required"` on a pointer field validates the pointer is non-nil; on a string validates non-empty. `omitempty` skips validation if the field is zero value. `dive` validates slice/map elements: `Tags []string `validate:"min=1,dive,min=2"`` validates each tag is at least 2 chars. For nested structs, validation is recursive automatically. Thread-safe — create one `validator.Validate` instance and reuse.

FAILURE: Go goroutine leak from abandoned channels with no receiver. Sending to a channel with no receiver blocks forever: `ch := make(chan int); go func() { ch <- expensiveResult() }()` — if the caller returns without receiving from `ch`, the goroutine leaks. Similarly, receiving from a channel no one writes to leaks. Common patterns causing this: fire-and-forget goroutines writing results to channels, timeout handling without cleanup, pipeline stages where a producer goroutine writes to a channel whose consumer exited early. Fix: always use context cancellation and select with done channel: `select { case ch <- result: case <-ctx.Done(): return }`. Detect leaks with `goleak` in tests: `defer goleak.VerifyNone(t)`. UNSAID: goroutines are cheap but not free — each costs ~2-8KB of stack. A service leaking 1 goroutine per request leaks ~4MB per 1000 requests. `runtime.NumGoroutine()` exposed via `/debug/vars` endpoint shows goroutine count trends. `pprof` goroutine profile shows all live goroutines with stack traces. The most dangerous leaks are in long-lived services where leaked goroutines accumulate indefinitely.

Go `gomock` vs `testify/mock` choice: `gomock` (google/mock) generates strict mocks from interfaces — unexpected calls fail immediately, all expected calls must occur. `testify/mock` is manually written, more flexible, allows partial mocking. `gomock` workflow: `//go:generate mockgen -source=service.go -destination=mock_service.go; ctrl := gomock.NewController(t); mock := NewMockService(ctrl); mock.EXPECT().GetUser(ctx, 123).Return(user, nil)`. `testify/mock` workflow: embed `mock.Mock`, implement methods calling `m.Called(args...)`, set expectations with `m.On("GetUser", ctx, 123).Return(user, nil)`. UNSAID: `gomock` is stricter and better for preventing over-mocking and catching unexpected calls — preferred for TDD. `testify/mock` is better when you need partial mocking or when the interface is large and you only mock a subset. `mockery` (vektra/mockery) auto-generates `testify/mock` implementations — combines generation with flexibility. For HTTP: `net/http/httptest` is better than mocking — test with a real HTTP server. For external services in integration tests: `testcontainers-go` over mocks.

Go `strings` and `bytes` packages performance: for building strings in a loop, always use `strings.Builder` — not `+=` concatenation (quadratic time). `strings.Contains(s, sub)` uses SIMD-optimized Boyer-Moore-Horspool. `strings.Split` vs `strings.SplitN`: use `SplitN` with a limit when you only need the first N parts. `strings.TrimSpace` removes Unicode whitespace. `bytes.Equal(a, b)` is equivalent to `string(a) == string(b)` but avoids allocation. `strings.NewReplacer` for multiple substitutions is faster than chained `strings.Replace` calls — build once, reuse. `strings.Fields` splits on any whitespace. UNSAID: converting between `string` and `[]byte` always allocates in current Go (except in specific compiler-optimized cases like map lookups). For read-only byte slice operations, use `strings` on the string directly. `unsafe.String` and `unsafe.SliceData` (1.20+) allow zero-copy conversion but require careful lifetime management. For regex: `regexp.MustCompile` at package initialization — never compile regex in a hot path. `regexp.Regexp` methods are goroutine-safe.

FAILURE: Go `http.ListenAndServe` without timeout configuration enabling Slowloris attacks. `http.ListenAndServe(addr, handler)` uses a server with no timeouts — slow or malicious clients can hold connections open indefinitely, exhausting file descriptors. Fix: always configure timeouts: `srv := &http.Server{Addr: addr, Handler: handler, ReadTimeout: 15*time.Second, WriteTimeout: 15*time.Second, IdleTimeout: 60*time.Second, ReadHeaderTimeout: 5*time.Second, MaxHeaderBytes: 1<<20}; srv.ListenAndServe()`. `ReadHeaderTimeout` is most important for Slowloris — it limits time to receive headers. `ReadTimeout` covers body read. `WriteTimeout` covers response write. UNSAID: for long-running streaming responses or WebSocket upgrades, `WriteTimeout` must be disabled or set to a large value — it interrupts long streams. `IdleTimeout` for keep-alive connections (clients reusing connections) — set to 2x client keep-alive interval. The default `http.DefaultServeMux` is not safe for production — use your own mux. Behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Envoy), TCP-level timeouts provide another layer, but application-level timeouts are still needed.

Go `grpc-go` server setup with interceptors: `s := grpc.NewServer(grpc.UnaryInterceptor(grpc_middleware.ChainUnaryServer(grpc_recovery.UnaryServerInterceptor(), grpc_zap.UnaryServerInterceptor(logger), grpc_auth.UnaryServerInterceptor(authFunc), otelgrpc.UnaryServerInterceptor())))`. Register service: `pb.RegisterUserServiceServer(s, &userServer{})`. Reflection for grpcurl debugging: `reflection.Register(s)`. Serve: `l, _ := net.Listen("tcp", ":50051"); s.Serve(l)`. UNSAID: interceptor order matters — recovery (panic handler) should be outermost, auth before business logic. `go-grpc-middleware/v2` provides production-ready interceptors. For streaming RPCs, use stream interceptors separately. gRPC health checking: `grpc_health_v1.RegisterHealthServer(s, health.NewServer())`. For load balancing in Kubernetes, use `grpc.WithDefaultServiceConfig({"loadBalancingConfig": [{"round_robin":{}}]})` on the client — gRPC connections are long-lived, so per-connection round-robin is needed to distribute across pod replicas. Use `keepalive.ServerParameters` to detect dead clients.

Go `go-migrate` for database schema migrations: `m, err := migrate.New("file://migrations", dsn)`. Up: `m.Up()`. Down: `m.Down()`. Steps: `m.Steps(2)`. Version: `version, dirty, err := m.Version()`. Migration files named `000001_create_users.up.sql` and `000001_create_users.down.sql`. In CI/CD: run migrations before deploying new application version. In code: `migrate.Up()` at application startup for automatic migration (suitable for non-production). UNSAID: `dirty` state means a migration failed partway — the database is in an inconsistent state. Fix: manually fix the database, then `m.Force(version)` to clear the dirty flag. Never use `Up()` in production automatically — human review and staged rollout is required. For PostgreSQL, each migration runs in a transaction by default — if any statement fails, the entire migration rolls back. Use advisory locks (via `WithDatabaseDriven`) to prevent concurrent migration runs in multi-instance deployments. `golang-migrate` supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, MongoDB, and more via driver plugins.

FAILURE: Go `encoding/gob` for cross-language serialization or versioned data. `gob` is Go-specific — no support in other languages. `gob`-encoded data is not human-readable and has no schema — adding, removing, or reordering fields can silently corrupt deserialization in subtle ways (field names must match exactly). For cross-service communication: use protobuf, JSON, or MessagePack. For persistent storage: JSON or protobuf with versioned schemas. UNSAID: `gob` is suitable for Go-to-Go RPC where both sides always update together (e.g., `net/rpc`), or for in-memory channel communication in a single binary. It is not suitable for any scenario requiring forward or backward compatibility without field-name coordination. `gob` sends field names in the encoded stream — renaming a field breaks decoding. Deleted fields in the sender are silently ignored by the receiver; added fields in the receiver receive zero values. These properties make `gob` appear to work during development but fail silently in production when clients and servers run different versions.

Go production logging with `slog` and structured output: `logger := slog.New(slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: slog.LevelInfo}))`. Log with context: `logger.InfoContext(ctx, "user created", "user_id", userID, "email", email)`. Add request ID to context for tracing: `ctx = context.WithValue(ctx, requestIDKey, reqID)`. Create child logger with fixed fields: `childLogger := logger.With("service", "user-service", "version", version)`. Levels: Debug, Info, Warn, Error. UNSAID: `slog` is in stdlib since Go 1.21 — prefer it over zap/logrus for new projects (reduces dependencies). `slog.SetDefault(logger)` sets the package-level logger for code using `slog.Info(...)`. For high-throughput logging, `slog.NewJSONHandler` is slightly slower than zap's JSON encoder — benchmark if logging is a bottleneck. Log sampling (log only 1% of debug messages at high volume): `slog` does not have built-in sampling; use a sampling handler wrapper. Avoid logging inside tight loops — batch log entries or log only on errors. Never log passwords, tokens, PII without masking.

Go `rate` package for token bucket rate limiting: `limiter := rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second), 10)` — 10 requests per second with burst of 10. Wait for a token (blocks): `limiter.Wait(ctx)`. Non-blocking check: `if !limiter.Allow() { http.Error(w, "rate limited", 429); return }`. Per-user rate limiting with a map: `limiters sync.Map` keyed by user ID, each with its own `rate.Limiter`. Clean up stale limiters with a periodic GC goroutine. UNSAID: `rate.Limiter` is in-memory — it resets on process restart and does not work across multiple instances. For distributed rate limiting, use Redis with sliding window algorithm: `go-redis/redis_rate` or `throttled/throttled`. Token bucket allows bursts up to the bucket size — `rate.NewLimiter(rate.Limit(10), 50)` allows 50 requests in one burst, then refills at 10/sec. `rate.Every(time.Millisecond*100)` is equivalent to 10/sec but with finer granularity. `limiter.Reserve()` returns a `*rate.Reservation` with delay information for more flexible scheduling.

Go `pgxpool` connection pool with health checking: `config, _ := pgxpool.ParseConfig(dsn); config.MaxConns = 20; config.MinConns = 5; config.MaxConnLifetime = time.Hour; config.MaxConnIdleTime = 30*time.Minute; config.HealthCheckPeriod = time.Minute; pool, _ := pgxpool.NewWithConfig(ctx, config)`. Acquire a connection: `conn, err := pool.Acquire(ctx); defer conn.Release()`. Or use pool directly: `pool.QueryRow(ctx, sql, args...)`. UNSAID: `pgxpool` is the preferred way to use PostgreSQL in production Go — it handles connection recycling, health checks, and parallel query support. `MaxConns` should not exceed `max_connections` in PostgreSQL minus connections reserved for admin/replication. Too many connections cause PostgreSQL to spend more time context-switching than executing queries — typically 20-100 connections per application instance. Use PgBouncer for connection pooling at the infrastructure level when running many instances. `pgxpool.Pool` is safe for concurrent use — no external synchronization needed. For transactions: `pool.BeginTx(ctx, pgx.TxOptions{IsoLevel: pgx.Serializable})`.

FAILURE: Go `interface{}` (or `any`) as map key without comparable constraint. Maps require keys to be comparable. Passing a slice, map, or function as a key panics at runtime: `m := map[any]int{}; m[[]int{1,2}] = 1` panics with `runtime error: hash of unhashable type []int`. With generics, this becomes a compile-time error: `func Count[K comparable](items []K) map[K]int`. The comparable constraint prevents non-comparable types at compile time. UNSAID: `comparable` in generics is stricter than the runtime comparability — interface types satisfy `comparable` at compile time but may panic at runtime if the underlying value is not comparable. This is a known Go spec nuance. For runtime-safe comparable checks: `reflect.TypeOf(v).Comparable()`. Common safe map key types: string, int, struct with only comparable fields, pointers (compared by address). Named struct types with all comparable fields are automatically comparable and make excellent map keys (e.g., `type CacheKey struct { UserID int64; ResourceType string }`).

Go `prometheus/client_golang` histogram for latency: `latencyHistogram := prometheus.NewHistogramVec(prometheus.HistogramOpts{Name: "http_request_duration_seconds", Help: "HTTP request latency", Buckets: prometheus.DefBuckets}, []string{"method","path","status"})`. Record: `timer := prometheus.NewTimer(latencyHistogram.WithLabelValues(method, path, status)); defer timer.ObserveDuration()`. `prometheus.DefBuckets` = `{0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10}` seconds. For SLO-oriented buckets targeting p99 < 200ms: `Buckets: []float64{0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0}`. UNSAID: histograms are expensive — high cardinality label combinations (e.g., per-user-ID labels) create millions of time series and crash Prometheus. Keep label cardinality low — use path patterns, not individual URLs (e.g., `/users/{id}` not `/users/123`). `prometheus.MustRegister` panics on duplicate registration — safe for package-level init but not for tests that run multiple times. Use `prometheus.NewRegistry()` for test isolation. For Kubernetes, use the `prometheus-community/helm-charts` for automatic pod scraping.

Go `spf13/viper` + `cobra` configuration hierarchy: Viper reads from (in priority order): explicit Set > flag > env var > config file > default. Bind cobra flag to viper: `viper.BindPFlag("port", cmd.Flags().Lookup("port"))`. Bind env vars with prefix: `viper.SetEnvPrefix("APP"); viper.AutomaticEnv()` — env `APP_DATABASE_URL` maps to key `database.url` with `viper.SetEnvKeyReplacer(strings.NewReplacer(".", "_"))`. Read config file: `viper.SetConfigName("config"); viper.AddConfigPath("/etc/app"); viper.AddConfigPath("."); viper.ReadInConfig()`. Unmarshal to struct: `viper.Unmarshal(&cfg)`. UNSAID: `viper.GetString` returns zero value if key not found — always validate required config after loading. Use `github.com/spf13/viper/remote` for etcd/Consul config watching. For twelve-factor apps (12factor.net), environment variables are the canonical config source — use `AutomaticEnv` and limit config files to development. `viper.WatchConfig()` hot-reloads config files — requires thread-safe config access in the application.

FAILURE: Go `panic` in goroutines not recovered in the goroutine itself. A panic in a goroutine that has no `recover` terminates the entire process — not just the goroutine. HTTP servers using `net/http` automatically recover panics in handler goroutines, but custom goroutines (worker pools, background tasks) do not have this protection. A panic in `go func() { processJob(job) }()` with no recover kills the whole service. Fix: wrap goroutine bodies with recover: `go func() { defer func() { if r := recover(); r != nil { log.Printf("recovered panic: %v", r) } }(); processJob(job) }()`. For worker pools: handle recovery in the worker loop, not in individual task functions. UNSAID: `recover` only works inside a deferred function in the same goroutine — you cannot recover a panic in another goroutine. Production services should log the stack trace on recovery: `debug.Stack()` from `runtime/debug`. Consider panic as a programming error (bug), not a control flow mechanism — excessive panic/recover patterns hide bugs. `grpc-go`'s `grpc_recovery` interceptor provides this for gRPC handlers. Never recover and continue silently — always log with stack trace.

Go `oauth2` and OIDC integration: `golang.org/x/oauth2` handles OAuth2 flows. For OIDC: `coreos/go-oidc/v3`. Provider discovery: `provider, _ := oidc.NewProvider(ctx, "https://accounts.google.com")`. OAuth2 config: `oauth2Config := oauth2.Config{ClientID: id, ClientSecret: secret, Endpoint: provider.Endpoint(), RedirectURL: callback, Scopes: []string{oidc.ScopeOpenID, "profile", "email"}}`. Auth URL: `url := oauth2Config.AuthCodeURL(state, oauth2.AccessTypeOffline)`. Token exchange: `token, _ := oauth2Config.Exchange(ctx, code)`. Verify ID token: `verifier := provider.Verifier(&oidc.Config{ClientID: id}); idToken, _ := verifier.Verify(ctx, token.Extra("id_token").(string))`. Extract claims: `idToken.Claims(&claims)`. UNSAID: always validate the `state` parameter to prevent CSRF. Use PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for public clients. Store tokens encrypted — never in plain cookies. Refresh token rotation: use `oauth2.Config.TokenSource` with automatic refresh. For machine-to-machine: client credentials flow. For service-to-service within a cluster: use service account tokens (Kubernetes SPIFFE/SPIRE).

Go `io` package composition for processing pipelines: chain readers with `io.TeeReader` (read and simultaneously write to another writer), `io.LimitReader` (limit bytes read), `io.MultiReader` (concatenate readers), `io.MultiWriter` (write to multiple writers). Compression: `gzip.NewReader(r)` / `gzip.NewWriter(w)`. Encryption: wrap with `cipher.StreamReader`. Hashing while reading: `tee := io.TeeReader(body, sha256Hasher); io.Copy(dst, tee); hash := sha256Hasher.Sum(nil)`. UNSAID: composing readers/writers avoids buffering entire payloads in memory — critical for large file processing (GB-scale). `io.Pipe()` connects a reader and writer with goroutine coordination — write to `PipeWriter` in one goroutine, read from `PipeReader` in another. `io.Copy(dst, src)` uses a 32KB internal buffer by default; `io.CopyBuffer(dst, src, buf)` allows providing your own buffer to reduce allocations. `bufio.Reader` adds buffering to any `io.Reader` — essential for line-by-line reading (`bufio.Scanner`). Always close readers that implement `io.Closer` — `http.Response.Body` being the most common example.

FAILURE: Go `net.Conn` read/write deadline not reset causing permanent timeout. `conn.SetDeadline(time.Now().Add(timeout))` sets an absolute deadline — after it fires, ALL future reads and writes on that connection return immediately with a timeout error, even after calling `SetDeadline` again with a new deadline. The connection is effectively permanently broken. Correct pattern: reset the deadline before each operation: `conn.SetDeadline(time.Now().Add(idleTimeout))` before each read, and `conn.SetDeadline(time.Now().Add(writeTimeout))` before each write. UNSAID: `SetReadDeadline` and `SetWriteDeadline` set independent deadlines. `SetDeadline(time.Time{})` (zero value) removes the deadline. This affects TCP connections, TLS connections, and UDP connections. For HTTP clients: `http.Client.Timeout` sets the total round-trip timeout cleanly. For raw TCP servers implementing protocols: deadline-per-operation is the correct pattern. Forgetting to reset the deadline is a common source of mysterious connection failures under load (when connections are reused from pools after being idle).

Go Kafka consumer with `segmentio/kafka-go` consumer groups: `r := kafka.NewReader(kafka.ReaderConfig{Brokers: brokers, GroupID: groupID, Topic: topic, MinBytes: 10e3, MaxBytes: 10e6, CommitInterval: time.Second, StartOffset: kafka.LastOffset})`. Read in loop: `for { m, err := r.ReadMessage(ctx); if err != nil { break }; process(m.Value); }`. Manual commit for at-least-once: use `r.FetchMessage(ctx)` then `r.CommitMessages(ctx, m)` after processing. Close: `defer r.Close()`. UNSAID: `CommitInterval: time.Second` enables auto-commit at intervals — messages may be reprocessed on restart if not committed. For exactly-once or strict at-least-once, use manual commit with `FetchMessage` + `CommitMessages`. Consumer group semantics: all readers with the same `GroupID` share partitions — rebalancing occurs when readers join/leave. `StartOffset: kafka.FirstOffset` re-processes from the beginning; `LastOffset` starts from new messages. For dead letter queues: on processing failure, write to a separate `_dlq` topic instead of retrying indefinitely.

Go production readiness checklist (non-functional requirements): structured JSON logging with request IDs; HTTP server with all timeouts configured (ReadHeader, Read, Write, Idle); graceful shutdown on SIGTERM draining in-flight requests; health check endpoints (/healthz liveness, /readyz readiness) on a management port; Prometheus metrics with RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration); distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry and W3C TraceContext propagation; circuit breakers on all downstream dependencies; retry with exponential backoff and jitter for transient failures; connection pool sizing tuned to backend limits; context propagation throughout the call stack; config from environment variables (12-factor); structured error types with HTTP status mapping; rate limiting per consumer; pprof endpoints behind auth on management port; Docker multi-stage build with non-root user; Kubernetes liveness/readiness probes; GOMAXPROCS set via `automaxprocs`; pre-commit hooks for lint and tests. UNSAID: each item addresses a class of production incidents — none are optional for services handling real traffic. Skip one and it becomes the root cause of a future outage.

Go `cmp` package (stdlib, 1.21+) for generic comparison: `cmp.Compare(a, b)` returns -1/0/1 for ordered types — replaces `if a < b { return -1 } else if a > b { return 1 } else { return 0 }`. `cmp.Or(a, b, c...)` returns the first non-zero value — replaces chained `if a != "" { return a }`. For testing deep equality: `google/go-cmp`'s `cmp.Diff(want, got)` produces human-readable diffs for test assertions. Options: `cmpopts.IgnoreFields(T{}, "CreatedAt")` to ignore timestamp fields in comparisons. UNSAID: `cmp.Or` (stdlib) is particularly useful for default value patterns: `addr := cmp.Or(os.Getenv("ADDR"), ":8080")`. The stdlib `cmp` package is minimal — it only provides `Compare` and `Or`. `google/go-cmp` is a separate dependency focused on test assertions and provides much richer comparison capabilities. `cmpopts.EquateEmpty()` treats nil and empty slices/maps as equal. `cmpopts.SortSlices(less)` sorts before comparing. These options are critical for deterministic test assertions on structs containing time.Time or generated IDs.

FAILURE: Go `time.Now()` in tests causing flaky assertions. Tests that compare timestamps using `time.Now()` directly are time-dependent and fail when execution is slow (CI under load, debug mode). `assert.Equal(t, time.Now(), record.CreatedAt)` almost always fails. Fix: use `cmpopts.EquateApproxTime(time.Second)` from `google/go-cmp` for approximate time comparison, or pass a clock interface: `type Clock interface { Now() time.Time }; type realClock struct{}; func (realClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }`. In tests: inject a fake clock with a fixed time. For database timestamps: compare only that `CreatedAt` is within a reasonable window of test start: `assert.WithinDuration(t, time.Now(), record.CreatedAt, 5*time.Second)`. UNSAID: this is a specific instance of the general principle that non-deterministic inputs (time, random numbers, external state) must be injectable for tests to be reliable. `testify`'s `assert.WithinDuration` is the simplest fix for database integration tests. For unit tests of time-sensitive logic, the clock interface pattern is essential. `jonboulle/clockwork` provides a ready-made fake clock implementation.

Go cursor-based pagination for large datasets: instead of `OFFSET/LIMIT` (which scans all previous rows), use a cursor (last seen ID or composite cursor): `SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE id > $1 ORDER BY id ASC LIMIT $2`. Return cursor: the last ID in the result set. Client passes cursor on next request. For compound sort keys: `WHERE (created_at, id) > ($1, $2)` using row value comparison (PostgreSQL, MySQL 8+). Encode cursor as opaque base64 to hide implementation details: `cursor := base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(json.Marshal(CursorData{ID: lastID, CreatedAt: lastCreatedAt}))`. UNSAID: `OFFSET` performance degrades linearly — at page 1000 with page size 20, PostgreSQL scans 20,000 rows. Cursor pagination is O(1) per page regardless of depth. Limitation: cursor pagination cannot jump to arbitrary pages — only next/previous. For search results requiring jump-to-page, use Elasticsearch or keyset pagination with search-engine backing. Cursor values must be opaque to clients — never expose raw IDs that reveal internal structure or enable ID enumeration.

Go `x/exp/maps` and `x/exp/slices` vs stdlib: before Go 1.21, experimental generic utilities lived in `golang.org/x/exp/maps` and `golang.org/x/exp/slices`. With Go 1.21, equivalent functions moved to stdlib `maps` and `slices` packages. Migration: replace `exp/slices` import with `slices`, `exp/maps` with `maps`. Key API differences: `slices.SortFunc` changed its comparator signature from `func(a, b E) bool` (less-than) to `func(a, b E) int` (three-way compare, returns negative/zero/positive) — a breaking change requiring code updates. UNSAID: `x/exp` packages are explicitly marked as experimental and may change API without notice — do not use in production libraries. The stdlib versions are stable. Check all callers of `SortFunc` when migrating — the boolean less-than comparator silently compiles with the int signature (returning int from bool comparison context) but produces wrong sort results. Migration: `func less(a, b T) bool { ... }` → `func cmp(a, b T) int { if less(a, b) { return -1 }; if less(b, a) { return 1 }; return 0 }`.

Go `http.RoundTripper` for HTTP client middleware: implement `RoundTrip(req *http.Request) (*http.Response, error)` to intercept all outbound HTTP requests. Common uses: add authentication headers, log requests/responses, add distributed tracing, retry logic, circuit breaking. Pattern: `type AuthTransport struct { Token string; Wrapped http.RoundTripper }; func (t *AuthTransport) RoundTrip(req *http.Request) (*http.Response, error) { req = req.Clone(req.Context()); req.Header.Set("Authorization", "Bearer "+t.Token); return t.Wrapped.RoundTrip(req) }`. Compose: `client := &http.Client{Transport: &AuthTransport{Token: token, Wrapped: &OtelTransport{Wrapped: http.DefaultTransport}}}`. UNSAID: always call `req.Clone(ctx)` before modifying the request — the original `*http.Request` must not be modified per `http.RoundTripper` contract. `http.DefaultTransport` is the baseline TCP transport — always wrap it rather than replacing entirely to preserve keep-alive and other optimizations. Chain transports from outermost to innermost: logging→auth→otel→http.DefaultTransport. `otelhttp.NewTransport(http.DefaultTransport)` from OpenTelemetry is the standard observability wrapper.

FAILURE: Go `context.Background()` passed to goroutines instead of the request context, breaking cancellation chains. When a goroutine is spawned inside an HTTP handler or service call, using `context.Background()` instead of the incoming `ctx` means the goroutine continues running even after the client disconnects or the timeout fires. This wastes resources on work whose result no one will use, and can cascade into database query pile-ups. Fix: always pass the parent context to spawned goroutines. For goroutines that outlive the request (e.g., background jobs), use a derived context from a service-level context: `svcCtx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())` tied to the service lifecycle. UNSAID: this is especially harmful for database queries — a `db.QueryContext(context.Background(), ...)` ignores client disconnection and holds DB connections until the query completes. Most production incidents from context misuse involve `context.Background()` in goroutines. The pattern: every goroutine entry point should receive a context as its first parameter — this forces the caller to think about the context lifecycle.

Go `net/http/httputil.ReverseProxy` for building API gateways and proxies: `proxy := &httputil.ReverseProxy{Director: func(req *http.Request) { req.URL.Scheme = "http"; req.URL.Host = backendAddr; req.Header.Set("X-Forwarded-For", req.RemoteAddr) }, ModifyResponse: func(resp *http.Response) error { resp.Header.Set("X-Proxied", "true"); return nil }, ErrorHandler: func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request, err error) { http.Error(w, "Bad Gateway", 502) }}`. For load balancing: rotate `req.URL.Host` in Director across a pool of backends. UNSAID: `httputil.ReverseProxy` does not handle connection pooling between proxy and backends — configure a custom `Transport` with `MaxIdleConnsPerHost` set to the expected connection rate. `Director` must not modify the original request — clone if needed. For WebSocket proxying: set `Upgrader` to handle the upgrade. Production proxy concerns: request ID forwarding, circuit breaking per backend, TLS termination, rate limiting — `ReverseProxy` provides the plumbing but not the policies. Consider Envoy or Traefik for production API gateway needs.

Go `sync.Once` vs `sync.OnceFunc` (1.21+) and `sync.OnceValue` (1.21+): `sync.Once.Do(f)` runs `f` exactly once, even if called concurrently. `sync.OnceFunc(f)` (1.21+) returns a zero-arg function that calls `f` at most once. `sync.OnceValue(f)` returns a function that calls `f` once and caches the return value. `sync.OnceValues(f)` returns a function that calls `f` once and caches two return values (value + error). Pattern for lazy singletons: `var getDB = sync.OnceValue(func() *sql.DB { db, _ := sql.Open("postgres", dsn); return db })`. Call: `db := getDB()`. UNSAID: `sync.Once` panics if `Do`'s function panics — the `Once` is considered done, and future calls to `Do` return without calling `f` again. Use `sync.OnceValues` for initialization that can fail: `var initDB, initErr = sync.OnceValues(func() (*sql.DB, error) { return sql.Open(...) })`. `once_cell`-style lazy initialization is now idiomatic in Go 1.21+ without third-party packages. For package-level singletons, `var instance = sync.OnceValue(func() *T { return &T{...} })` is cleaner than `var once sync.Once; var instance *T`.

FAILURE: Go `append` in goroutines on a shared slice without synchronization. Multiple goroutines appending to the same slice concurrently cause data races — the race detector reports them, and results are non-deterministic. Even without apparent sharing, passing a slice value (header copy) to a goroutine and appending in both the caller and goroutine races on the underlying array if it hasn't been reallocated. Fix: use channels to collect results from goroutines, use a mutex-protected slice, or pre-allocate fixed-size results and write to specific indices (safe if each goroutine writes to a disjoint range). UNSAID: this is one of the most common Go data races in new concurrent code. The race detector (`go test -race`) catches it — always run with `-race` in CI. Pre-allocating: `results := make([]Result, len(inputs)); for i, input := range inputs { go func(i int, input T) { results[i] = process(input) }(i, input) }` — safe only if each goroutine writes to a distinct index. For fan-in patterns: `resultCh := make(chan Result, len(inputs)); for _, input := range inputs { go func(input T) { resultCh <- process(input) }(input) }`.

Go `testing/fstest.MapFS` for testing file system operations without disk I/O: `fsys := fstest.MapFS{"config.yaml": {Data: []byte("key: value")}, "templates/base.html": {Data: []byte("<html>...</html>")}}`. Pass to functions accepting `fs.FS`: `LoadConfig(fsys, "config.yaml")`. Test `go:embed` consumers without actual files. `fstest.TestFS(fsys, fileNames...)` validates that the FS implementation correctly implements the `fs.FS` interface contract. UNSAID: `fs.FS` (Go 1.16+) is the standard interface for read-only file systems — functions should accept `fs.FS` rather than string paths to be testable. `embed.FS`, `os.DirFS`, `fstest.MapFS`, and third-party implementations all satisfy `fs.FS`. Migrating from `ioutil.ReadFile(path)` to `fs.ReadFile(fsys, path)` enables complete I/O independence in tests. For writable file systems in tests: use `os.TempDir()` or `t.TempDir()` (automatically cleaned up). `t.TempDir()` (1.15+) creates a unique temp directory for each test and cleans it up in `t.Cleanup`.

Go `x/net/websocket` vs `gorilla/websocket` vs `nhooyr/websocket`: `gorilla/websocket` is the most widely used, but is in maintenance mode (no new features). `nhooyr/websocket` (also called `coder/websocket`) is actively maintained and has a cleaner API. Connection upgrade: `conn, err := websocket.Accept(w, r, &websocket.AcceptOptions{InsecureSkipVerify: false})`. Read: `msgType, reader, err := conn.Reader(ctx)`. Write: `w, _ := conn.Writer(ctx, websocket.MessageText); w.Write(data); w.Close()`. Ping/pong for keepalive: handled automatically. UNSAID: WebSocket connections bypass the HTTP request lifecycle — you must implement your own authentication, rate limiting, and timeout logic. Implement ping/pong to detect dead connections: if a client silently drops (network partition), without ping/pong the server goroutine and memory leak indefinitely. Set `SetReadDeadline` to detect clients not responding to pings. Each WebSocket connection consumes one goroutine — for 10,000 concurrent connections, consider `eranyanay/1m-go-websockets` epoll-based approach to reduce goroutine count.

Go `database/sql` transaction patterns: begin: `tx, err := db.BeginTx(ctx, &sql.TxOptions{Isolation: sql.LevelReadCommitted})`. Execute within tx: `tx.ExecContext(ctx, ...)`, `tx.QueryContext(ctx, ...)`. Commit or rollback: `if err != nil { tx.Rollback(); return err }; return tx.Commit()`. Helper to ensure rollback: `defer func() { if p := recover(); p != nil { tx.Rollback(); panic(p) } else if err != nil { tx.Rollback() } else { err = tx.Commit() } }()`. For nested operations: pass `*sql.Tx` to functions that need transaction participation — no automatic savepoints. UNSAID: `sql.LevelDefault` uses the database default isolation level (usually Read Committed for PostgreSQL, Repeatable Read for MySQL). For financial operations: use `sql.LevelSerializable` and handle `40001` serialization failure errors with retry. `tx.Rollback()` after `tx.Commit()` is safe — rollback on a committed transaction is a no-op. Always set `sql.TxOptions.ReadOnly: true` for read-only transactions — allows databases to optimize (no WAL writes, can route to replicas). Long-running transactions hold locks — keep transactions short and avoid network calls inside them.
